Attys. ANDREW M. DANAS, Esq. of Grove, Jaskiewicz and Cobert LLP (Tel: 202-296-2900) and JAMES SANSONE, ESQ. of Carle, Mackie, Power & Ross LLP (Tel: 707-526-4200) appearing for Defendants/Cross-Complainant Elaine Heather Hartshorn.

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF PACIFIC WINE DISTRIBUTORS, INC.'S Motion To Compel Defendant Elaine Heather Hartshorns Further Responses To Plaintiffs Request For Admissions, Set Two; Request For Sanctions In The Amount Of $1,545.00.

Parties stipulate to the matter being decided by the judge pro tem. Counsel and the judge pro tem appearing remotely authorize the clerk to sign the stipulation form on their behalf.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling: The motion to compel is DENIED. Moving party did not introduce evidence of whether "Shannon Burke" was a defined term, and if so, how it was defined. Absent such evidence, Hartshorn's statement of inability to admit or deny is well taken because there is a legal distinction between Burke as an individual and Burke Wine Brokerage as an organization, and it is unclear whether Hartshorn is able to admit or deny information about how Burke (either the individual or the principal of the brokerage) considered clients. Hartshorn's request for sanction is DENIED. The responses include boilerplate and irrelevant objections. Boilerplate objections are inappropriate and "may be sanctionable." Korea Data Systems Co. v. Sup. Ct. (1997) 51 Cal.App.4th 1513, 1516. Boilerplate objections that object "to the extent" a request seeks irrelevant or information documents are particularly problematic because it is not clear whether any information is being withheld based on a claim of relevance or privilege. Moreover, even if I were to disregard the objections, the statement of lack of knowledge sufficient to respond was not fully compliant with CCP 2033.220(c) because it did not state that a reasonable inquiry was made and that the information known or readily obtainable was insufficient. While I deny the motion based on the information provided in the moving papers, I decline to award sanctions because to do so would be unjust. CCP 2033.290(d).

Prevailing party to prepare and submit a proposed order to the judge pro tem.

Judge Pro Tem: Scott Borrowman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/JPT)
